summary: Patrick McKenzie reveals that he organized an anonymous working group in Tokyo that, by March 25, 2020, had concluded Japan was experiencing a widespread coronavirus epidemic despite the public consensus that Japan was handling the situation well. The group pre-registered their findings via cryptographic hashes on Twitter, then circulated a white paper through economist Tyler Cowen and other channels to accelerate policy response. Their core prediction—that Japan would face a public health crisis in April comparable to peer nations—proved correct, with Japan eventually declaring a nationwide state of emergency. McKenzie reflects on the institutional failures that allowed the epidemic to go undetected, particularly how Japan's cluster-based testing strategy (Plan A) was structurally incapable of detecting its own failure due to insufficient testing of asymptomatic patients outside known clusters.
key_points: Japan's cluster containment strategy (Plan A) was structurally blind to its own failure because it concentrated testing on symptomatic patients near known clusters, missing widespread asymptomatic community transmission
A group of four non-expert professionals in Tokyo produced a correct epidemiological forecast in 54 hours by synthesizing publicly available data, official documents, and epidemiology papers

The concept of 'sontaku'—intuiting and facilitating the preferences of other parts of a system without explicit instruction—explains institutional inertia better than conspiracy theories about sporting events
Pre-registering predictions via cryptographic hashes served both as scientific rigor and as a coded signal to trusted networks that something urgent was discovered
Too many people and organizations lost precious days waiting for permission to act on evidence they already had, suggesting epistemic humility can be invoked to dangerous excess during crises
money_quotes: It may not have been anyone's job to wake up every morning, assume Plan A was no longer viable, and search for proof that that had happened.
Imagine how surprised you would be if someone turned into a cat in front of you. That was how surprised I was.
There are few conspiracies in the world. There are many systems with complicated decision-making processes, internal data flows, and incentive structures for actors within them.
No professional courtesy, no personal discomfort, no preservation of modesty, no fear of repercussions should delay the response. Get a right enough answer. Make a right enough decision.
The alarm, buttressing training delivered far before the alarm rings, gives you unquestionable and immediate permission to evacuate.
As a nation, we believe we are in a peaceful spring. We are not.
The virus will not wait. Humanity must act faster than the disaster.
Too many people and too many organizations have passed precious days this year waiting for permission to take actions reasonable given evidence they already had.
key_insight: Systems designed to monitor a specific strategy can be structurally incapable of detecting that strategy's failure, and institutional inertia means no one may be assigned the job of looking for evidence that the current plan has already collapsed.
